cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ن ↔ He נ; Ar ب ↔ He ב; Ar أ ↔ He א — typical correspondence for the root n-b-ʔ
cognates.notes: While the Arabic root appears final-weak (*n-b-y) in some traditional analyses due to the surface form nabi, it is commonly identified as a loanword from or cognate with the Hebrew root *n-b-ʔ (related to Akkadian nabû 'to call').

cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ن ↔ Akk n; Ar ب ↔ Akk b; Ar أ ↔ Akk ∅/ʔ — standard correspondence
cognates.notes: Semantic development from 'called/proclaimed' to 'prophet' is well-attested in the West Semitic path.

caveats: v0 prototype: this is an LLM-generated analysis, not a lexicon lookup. Results may be incomplete or incorrect, especially for Syriac, Tigrinya, and rare forms. Verify against Wiktionary or a scholarly lexicon before citing.

The etymology of 'nabi' is highly debated; it is often considered a loanword in Arabic from a West Semitic (likely Aramaic or Hebrew) source due to the irregular behavior of the third root consonant which does not follow standard Arabic final-weak patterns.
